EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms tracked across all of eastern Iowa during the late afternoon and evening of Saturday March 5, 2022. Thunderstorms initially developed in southwest Iowa, and moved across central Iowa during the afternoon hours before reaching, our area after 600 PM. Initially, storms produced large hail and tornadoes over central Iowa, before the threat evolved more into damaging winds and tornadoes as storms moved into eastern Iowa and northwest Illinois. Winds around 60-75 MPH were reported from trained spotters and first responders, with confirmed reports of damage in some locations. EF-1 tornadoes occurred near Davenport and Vinton.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An off duty NWS employee reported that the housing for a traffic light at the intersection of Division and 76th Streets were physically turned by strong thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front swept across the area on Friday March 25th, with strong gusty winds developing behind the front. There were widespread wind speeds of 20 to 30 MPH with gusts up to 45 MPH. Rain showers developed behind the cold front with reported wind gusts in these showers of 50 to 60 MPH during the afternoon and evening hours. There was a wind gust of 67 mph at the Cedar Rapids Airport. Winds began to diminish after 10 PM.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS at the Davenport Municipal Airport measured a peak gust of 59 MPH in a rain shower.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Initial storms developed within the warm sector of a surface low that tracked northwest of the area, followed by a strong cold front with associated dry line that produced a quasi-linear (QLCS) storm mode later in the evening. Several tornadoes occurred across western into northern Iowa.  Initially the tornadoes were associated with supercell storms, however later storms produced QLCS tornadoes as it raced east northeast across the state. In addition to the tornadoes, hail up to tennis ball size occurred with initial super cells. The later QLCS produced hail up to golf ball sized along with damaging wind gusts over 70 mph at times.||Additionally, total rainfall in excess of 2 to 4 inches fell in a swath across areas that dealt with the severe thunderstorm and tornado warnings earlier in the event. While no flash flooding was experienced, rainfall in the Winnebago River basin above Mason City resulted in minor river flooding on April 13th, exceeding minor flood stage in the morning and receding below flood stage that evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Carroll AWOS gusted to 70 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: There was barely a break between the exit of the earlier morning severe convection, and development of the next round during the later morning and early afternoon on May 30th across southeast South Dakota.  A series of impulses moved northeast during the day, with a leading strong wave moving out of Nebraska by early afternoon. Storms blossomed near a cold frontal boundary toward the James River Valley, despite the limited diurnal destabilization, due to steep mid-level lapse rates which brought MLCAPE up to 1500-2000J/kg in the warm sector. Strong mid-level winds led to long, linear hodographs, but strong deep-layer forcing eventually resulted in an evolution toward linear structures with mainly damaging wind.  Near the core of the upper level wave, a secondary band of thunderstorms developed across Nebraska during the late afternoon and raced northeastward through early evening, featuring mainly damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind-driven dime size hail caused some damage to vegetation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A volatile environment featured a warm front from parts of east central South Dakota into western Minnesota, with a dryline extending to the southwest into Nebraska. With a very strong wave moving northeast through Nebraska into a highly unstable and highly sheared environment, scattered storms initiated and quickly grew upscale to a broad linear structure with damaging winds. With crop development well behind schedule and many fields yet to be planted, there was ample dirt available to be lofted into the leading edge of the storms, which took on the characteristics of a haboob reducing visibility to near zero at the onset of high winds.||A broad swath of winds from 70 to 100 mph devastated much of southeast South Dakota, and portions of southwest Minnesota, northeast Nebraska and northwest Iowa, causing extensive tree and structural damage and scores of injuries. The highest observed wind gust was 107 mph near Tripp, SD. In addition, vehicles and high profile vehicles were blown off several roads, shutting down traffic on Interstates 29 and 90. Two (direct) fatalities occurred in vehicles impacted by debris as the storms moved into the Sioux Falls area. Power was disrupted in a widespread area, with estimates of over 45,000 customers impacted at one time. Many schools were closed due to damage and power issues. The South Dakota Governor requested and was granted a Presidential Disaster Declaration for damage to public infrastructure at an estimated cost of 6.7 million dollars across 20 counties and two reservations. Private losses were a much higher toll, and just one of the impacted counties (McCook) estimated at least 150 to 175 million dollars in damage. Presidential Disaster Declarations were also granted in Dixon County, Nebraska and Lincoln County, Minnesota.||Following the afternoon line of storms, a secondary band of storms developed near the core of the upper level jet across eastern Nebraska in the early evening hours. These storms also quickly become severe, with a mix of large hail, damaging wind, and even an isolated tornado near Sioux City. This cluster of storms moved through far southeast South Dakota, northwest Iowa and southwest Minnesota during the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm wind gust of 58 mph was measured by Iowa Road Weather Information System site RSCI4 1 mile southwest of Moville. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed along a cold front in the early morning hours, with quarter sized hail and a few stronger winds gusts reported in central Iowa around sunrise. This pushed east of the area by mid morning and temperatures warmed. This allowed for deep mixing and non-thunderstorm associated wind gusts of 50 to over 60 mph occurred across the area through the afternoon. In the evening a secondary front passed across the state, with daytime heating allowing for more robust convection to develop. The bowing, linear storm system primarily produced a swath of wind gusts and damage, however a few smaller hail reports were made.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple tree limbs down via broadcast media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough dropped out of the Canadian Rockies and across the upper midwest. Storms developed along a surface boundary in the afternoon, resulting in a few hail reports and a strong wind gust. In addition to the moderate CAPE , the backed wind profile supported some rotation within storms. While no tornadoes occurred there was a funnel cloud reported north of the Des Moines metro.  Later that night the deep warm cloud layer and high precipitable water values made for efficient rain producers. As storms continued overnight along a stationary boundary locally heavy rain resulted in flash flooding in central Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: AWOS recorded a 51 knot gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed along a surface boundary across Nebraska and into western Iowa in the pre-dawn early morning hours of July 11th. The storms had well established cold pools as they moved into Iowa and an area of favorable MUCAPE which helped to sustain the convection. Storms produced over 70 mph in western Iowa, weakening some into central Iowa with sub-severe wind gusts by the time the storms reached eastern Iowa.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of showers and thunderstorms tracked east across Iowa Tuesday afternoon and evening bringing damaging wind, occasional lightning, and heavy rain to eastern IA and northwest IL.  The strongest reported winds of 60-70 mph, occurred in Buchanan and Linn counties.  These winds brought down trees and powerlines as they moved through in the evening.||A brief tornado occurred during the evening hours just southeast of Fairbank in an open field. Another brief tornado occurred just west of Winthrop IA in an open field.  Since no visible damage was observed with either tornado, they were assigned an EF-U rating.  The NWS would like to thank Buchanan County Emergency Management for assistance with the storm survey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Emergency Manager reported power lines down south of Tipton. The time of the event was estimated using radar.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reported two inch branches down due to thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms tracked across portions of eastern Iowa during the evening hours of July 23rd  and into northwest Illinois during the early morning of Sunday July 24th. Storms produced mainly damaging winds along their path, with numerous reports of structure, tree, and power line damage from Buchanan County Iowa east into Jo Daviess County Illinois. The Dubuque County Airport measured a 67 MPH wind gust as storms passed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Emergency Manager reported downed power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms tracked across portions of eastern Iowa during the evening hours of July 23rd  and into northwest Illinois during the early morning of Sunday July 24th. Storms produced mainly damaging winds along their path, with numerous reports of structure, tree, and power line damage from Buchanan County Iowa east into Jo Daviess County Illinois. The Dubuque County Airport measured a 67 MPH wind gust as storms passed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Dubuque Airport ASOS measured a peak thunderstorm wind gust of 58 knots.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave pushed across the Midwest, resulting a a period of severe storms. the leading moisture axis and warm front were confined main to Minnesota in the afternoon, though a few stronger storms skimmed northern Iowa with a hail report in Winnebago county. The cold front passed across Iowa later in the evening from western to northern Iowa. Modest CAPE was in place but with strong deep layer shear over 40 kts and drier low levels noted in model soundings, gusty winds were the primary hazard with this second round of thunderstorms. Winds gusted over 60 mph with a few reports of damage to trees and outbuildings.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter estimated 60 mph gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of thunderstorms developed across eastern Montana and the western Dakotas during the morning hours. These storms organized and turned southeastward, feeding on rich inflow of post-frontal air with up to 3000 J/kg of MLCAPE and over 1000 J/kg of DCAPE. Along with strong storm-relative shear, parameters favored development of a strong cold pool and balanced mesocyclone, which spread southeast with the cluster and developed a strong and long-duration severe wind event which traversed South Dakota, southwest Minnesota, extreme northeast Nebraska, and northwest Iowa during the afternoon and early evening. The storm was classified as a derecho, and featured numerous wind gusts from 70 to 100 mph and considerable damage to trees, crops, powerlines, and structures along its path.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms winds snapped a 150 foot tree in Sutherland - at the time the largest tree in O'Brien County.  Limbs which fell on powerlines resulted in up to 5 hours without power.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A derecho swept across South Dakota and into Iowa on the evening of July 5th. This resulted in a swath of  wind gust from 50 to 65 plus mph resulting in a number of power outages, tree damage, and a few damaged structures. A brief tornado occurred near Estherville with minor damage. As the bowing thunderstorm complex reach central Iowa the gusts had dropped below severe criteria given the less favorable environment with decreased deep layer shear.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Personal weather station recorded a 59 mph gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of storms produced damaging wind gusts and hail across southwest Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Coop Observer estimated wind gusts at 70 mph and visibility down to 2 miles due to dust. Also reported dime sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of storms produced damaging wind gusts and hail across southwest Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported trees uprooted or snapped.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe storms moved across northeast Iowa during the evening of April 12th. These storms produced five tornadoes and damaging wind gusts as high as 72 mph in Nora Springs (Floyd County). EF1 tornadoes occurred near Brownville (Mitchell County) and south of Riceville in both Mitchell and Howard Counties with both tornadoes damaging farm buildings and trees. EF0 tornadoes touched down in northern Floyd County into southern Mitchell County west of Orchard, near Saratoga (Howard County), and close to Ridgeway (Winneshiek County) with damage limited to trees and a few farm buildings. The Governor of Iowa issued a disaster proclamation for Howard, Mitchell, and Winneshiek Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A farm outbuilding had the roof and two walls blown off southwest of Ridgeway.

EF0 tornadoes touched down in northern Floyd County into southern Mitchell County west of Orchard, near Saratoga (Howard County), and close to Ridgeway (Winneshiek County) with damage limited to trees and a few farm buildings. The Governor of Iowa issued a disaster proclamation for Howard, Mitchell, and Winneshiek Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large farm outbuilding had part of the roof and walls blown off east of Ridgeway.

The later QLCS produced hail up to golf ball sized along with damaging wind gusts over 70 mph at times.||Additionally, total rainfall in excess of 2 to 4 inches fell in a swath across areas that dealt with the severe thunderstorm and tornado warnings earlier in the event. While no flash flooding was experienced, rainfall in the Winnebago River basin above Mason City resulted in minor river flooding on April 13th, exceeding minor flood stage in the morning and receding below flood stage that evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Personal weather station sustained at 54 mph and gusted to 75 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe storms moved across northeast Iowa during the evening of April 12th. These storms produced five tornadoes and damaging wind gusts as high as 72 mph in Nora Springs (Floyd County). EF1 tornadoes occurred near Brownville (Mitchell County) and south of Riceville in both Mitchell and Howard Counties with both tornadoes damaging farm buildings and trees. EF0 tornadoes touched down in northern Floyd County into southern Mitchell County west of Orchard, near Saratoga (Howard County), and close to Ridgeway (Winneshiek County) with damage limited to trees and a few farm buildings. The Governor of Iowa issued a disaster proclamation for Howard, Mitchell, and Winneshiek Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An old garage was pushed over by winds northeast of Ridgeway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep surface low moved north of the area, setting up a tight pressure gradient in the southwest quadrant of the low across Iowa. Dry air allowed for deep mixing and gusty winds aloft to be mixed down. Winds were breezy much f the day, with many sites experiencing winds sustained at 40 mph and a few areas gusting over 60 mph.

Initial storms developed within the warm sector of a surface low that tracked northwest of the area, followed by a strong cold front with associated dry line that produced a quasi-linear (QLCS) storm mode later in the evening. Several tornadoes occurred across western into northern Iowa.  Initially the tornadoes were associated with supercell storms, however later storms produced QLCS tornadoes as it raced east northeast across the state. In addition to the tornadoes, hail up to tennis ball size occurred with initial super cells. The later QLCS produced hail up to golf ball sized along with damaging wind gusts over 70 mph at times.||Additionally, total rainfall in excess of 2 to 4 inches fell in a swath across areas that dealt with the severe thunderstorm and tornado warnings earlier in the event. While no flash flooding was experienced, rainfall in the Winnebago River basin above Mason City resulted in minor river flooding on April 13th, exceeding minor flood stage in the morning and receding below flood stage that evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gust measured by algona AWOS.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: There was barely a break between the exit of the earlier morning severe convection, and development of the next round during the later morning and early afternoon on May 30th across southeast South Dakota.  A series of impulses moved northeast during the day, with a leading strong wave moving out of Nebraska by early afternoon. Storms blossomed near a cold frontal boundary toward the James River Valley, despite the limited diurnal destabilization, due to steep mid-level lapse rates which brought MLCAPE up to 1500-2000J/kg in the warm sector. Strong mid-level winds led to long, linear hodographs, but strong deep-layer forcing eventually resulted in an evolution toward linear structures with mainly damaging wind.  Near the core of the upper level wave, a secondary band of thunderstorms developed across Nebraska during the late afternoon and raced northeastward through early evening, featuring mainly damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few moderate-sized tree limbs were downed by thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A boundary moving across the state allowed for a cluster of thunderstorms to develop north of the Minnesota Iowa border in the morning and continue south southeast into Iowa through the afternoon. The overall convective parameter was not the most conducive to severe criteria thanks mostly to fairly weak forcing, however as the storms approached Zearing, Iowa the ingredients came together just right. The storm quickly strengthened, resulting in golf ball sized wind driven hail. This caused significant roof, siding, and tree damage in a swath through town.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread one to two inch diameter tree limbs down with larger old branches down. A few windows in town were blown out due to wind driven hail and hail was piled several inches deep in a few locations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a hot day with temperatures nearing 100 degrees at many locations, a front passed across the area overnight and brought scattered convection to the area through the morning hours of August 3rd. While most of this activity did remain sub severe, a thunderstorm wind gust of 66 mph was recorded in Sac County associated with outflow from a thunderstorm passing to the north.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station ERYI4 Early Iowa RWIS. Outflow from thunderstorm to the north.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of early morning storms developed on the nose of steeper mid-level lapse rates and marginally strong elevated wind shear across far southeast South Dakota. Multicell convection produced spotty large hail moving into southwest Minnesota. During the afternoon, convection re-ignited near an advancing mesoscale convective vortex and moved northeast from eastern Nebraska into southwest Minnesota.  With greater surface-based instability with a MLCAPE of 2000-3000 J/kg and deep-layer shear of 40-50 knots, storms quickly organized into a QLCS structure and produced hail up to ping-pong ball size, wind to 80 mph, and a few tornadoes across southwest Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree limbs were knocked down by strong thunderstorm wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold core low moved over the area, dragging along a weak and slow moving surface low. Although weak, sufficient low level and upper forcing aided in developing strong to severe storms. Instability and shear were strong enough for a few supercell thunderstorms to develop and bring hail, strong winds, and heavy rain to the area. The storms were slow moving. which allowed some storms to anchor over locations for an extended period of time, resulting in flash flooding. Some repeated storms over the same area also led to similar results. Severe weather reports were mostly in the form of hail, as these storms produced hail very efficiently. The largest hail reported was 1.5, and there were some isolated reports of damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Amateur radio relayed a report from law enforcement that trees were down on the north side of Conesville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A volatile environment featured a warm front from parts of east central South Dakota into western Minnesota, with a dryline extending to the southwest into Nebraska. With a very strong wave moving northeast through Nebraska into a highly unstable and highly sheared environment, scattered storms initiated and quickly grew upscale to a broad linear structure with damaging winds. With crop development well behind schedule and many fields yet to be planted, there was ample dirt available to be lofted into the leading edge of the storms, which took on the characteristics of a haboob reducing visibility to near zero at the onset of high winds.||A broad swath of winds from 70 to 100 mph devastated much of southeast South Dakota, and portions of southwest Minnesota, northeast Nebraska and northwest Iowa, causing extensive tree and structural damage and scores of injuries. The highest observed wind gust was 107 mph near Tripp, SD. In addition, vehicles and high profile vehicles were blown off several roads, shutting down traffic on Interstates 29 and 90. Two (direct) fatalities occurred in vehicles impacted by debris as the storms moved into the Sioux Falls area. Power was disrupted in a widespread area, with estimates of over 45,000 customers impacted at one time. Many schools were closed due to damage and power issues. The South Dakota Governor requested and was granted a Presidential Disaster Declaration for damage to public infrastructure at an estimated cost of 6.7 million dollars across 20 counties and two reservations. Private losses were a much higher toll, and just one of the impacted counties (McCook) estimated at least 150 to 175 million dollars in damage. Presidential Disaster Declarations were also granted in Dixon County, Nebraska and Lincoln County, Minnesota.||Following the afternoon line of storms, a secondary band of storms developed near the core of the upper level jet across eastern Nebraska in the early evening hours. These storms also quickly become severe, with a mix of large hail, damaging wind, and even an isolated tornado near Sioux City. This cluster of storms moved through far southeast South Dakota, northwest Iowa and southwest Minnesota during the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed large branches and powerlines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed in the early morning hours of April 23, 2022 within an area of elevated instability mainly along and east of Interstate 29. A couple thunderstorms produced marginally severe hail and damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed a few 6- to 9-inch diameter tree limbs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intense low pressure continued to produce a strong gradient across the Northern Plains, which intensified with another pulse of cold advection on April 14. Numerous high-profile vehicles had difficulty navigating the extreme wind gusts, with a few overturned semi-trucks across northwest Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds were frequently between 40 and 50 mph at Automated Weather Observing System site KSXK near Maurice, with several peak gusts of 58 mph measured at 1155CST, 1335CST and 1435CST.  A one foot diameter tree was snapped around 1200CST in Hull, and an awning torn off a house in Orange City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed across portions of eastern Iowa, northwest Illinois, and far northeast Missouri on the evening of September 18, 2022. Storms initially developed near the Iowa/Missouri border, with one storm near Bloomfield IA producing a well defined wall cloud before weakening. Storms developed and increased in strength just east of the Cedar Rapids and Iowa City metros as they slowly tracked east around the Interstate 80 corridor. Several large hail reports were received around Durant to south of Tipton, with the largest report being 2.5 inches. Storms also produced small hail and wind damage in the Quad Cities before spreading southeast into Henry County IL. Additional storms developed along the Iowa/Missouri/Illinois tri-state area, with several tornado warnings issued. Damage was reported in southern Hancock County near Tioga, and continued south into Adams County, although no tornadoes were confirmed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three inch diameter branch down due to the wind. The time was estimated from radar.

Damage was reported in southern Hancock County near Tioga, and continued south into Adams County, although no tornadoes were confirmed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Delayed report of trees down on Linden Lane and along Spruce Hill Drive. Time estimated from radar and previous report.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very strong deep-layer shear developed as a result of an increase in the low-level jet and turning mid-level winds ahead of the approaching strong upper-level trough. A strong push of mid-level instability created up to 1000 J/kg MUCAPE, despite an environment with a strong sub-cloud dry layer. Storms moved rapidly northeast, with a cluster of hail producing storms in east central South Dakota. A second round of wind-producing storms erupted on the nose of the low-level jet across far northeast Nebraska, and translated quickly through southeast South Dakota into northwest Iowa and southwest Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three-inch diameter tree branches were downed by thunderstorm wind gusts.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusts were measured by a personal weather station.

A second round of wind-producing storms erupted on the nose of the low-level jet across far northeast Nebraska, and translated quickly through southeast South Dakota into northwest Iowa and southwest Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous one to three-inch tree limbs were broken off by thunderstorm wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A volatile environment featured a warm front from parts of east central South Dakota into western Minnesota, with a dryline extending to the southwest into Nebraska. With a very strong wave moving northeast through Nebraska into a highly unstable and highly sheared environment, scattered storms initiated and quickly grew upscale to a broad linear structure with damaging winds. With crop development well behind schedule and many fields yet to be planted, there was ample dirt available to be lofted into the leading edge of the storms, which took on the characteristics of a haboob reducing visibility to near zero at the onset of high winds.||A broad swath of winds from 70 to 100 mph devastated much of southeast South Dakota, and portions of southwest Minnesota, northeast Nebraska and northwest Iowa, causing extensive tree and structural damage and scores of injuries. The highest observed wind gust was 107 mph near Tripp, SD. In addition, vehicles and high profile vehicles were blown off several roads, shutting down traffic on Interstates 29 and 90. Two (direct) fatalities occurred in vehicles impacted by debris as the storms moved into the Sioux Falls area. Power was disrupted in a widespread area, with estimates of over 45,000 customers impacted at one time. Many schools were closed due to damage and power issues. The South Dakota Governor requested and was granted a Presidential Disaster Declaration for damage to public infrastructure at an estimated cost of 6.7 million dollars across 20 counties and two reservations. Private losses were a much higher toll, and just one of the impacted counties (McCook) estimated at least 150 to 175 million dollars in damage. Presidential Disaster Declarations were also granted in Dixon County, Nebraska and Lincoln County, Minnesota.||Following the afternoon line of storms, a secondary band of storms developed near the core of the upper level jet across eastern Nebraska in the early evening hours. These storms also quickly become severe, with a mix of large hail, damaging wind, and even an isolated tornado near Sioux City. This cluster of storms moved through far southeast South Dakota, northwest Iowa and southwest Minnesota during the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mesonet station 3 miles north-northeast of Larchwood measured a wind gust of 87 mph.
